Huxter Family & Descendants Tree

Huxter Family & Descendants Tree Nfld----Canada

The Family tree starts in Kent, England and stops at Springdale, Nfld with Branches across Canada.

Map Of Newfoundland


The Huxter’s came from Luxembourg to Kent England sometime before 1580. Kent and Essex is north west of London. The family later moved to Bridport, south east coast of England.

Huxter-a surname of England, from middle English-to bargain hence a seller of small goods. Peddler—Hawker.

Bridport stands where the river Brit and Asker meet at the westerly end of Dorset. The region’s rich soil and temperate climate proved ideal for cultivation and production of flax and hemp, two crops which had a profound influence on Bridport’s prosperity.

Bridport Harbour prospered from its fishing and maritime trade. Some Bridport merchants deliberately competed against colonial suppliers to Newfoundland by assembling cargoes of goods to Virginia, USA. Bridport traded with Newfoundland and Virginia from the 16th century to the mid-19th century. Bridport ship carried fishnets, twine and rope to Newfoundland and Virginia, returned with fish, timber and tobacco. Records show that a salt house existed at Bridport to store salt for Newfoundland cod fisheries. Supplies of line, rope and sailcloth were taken from Bridport harbor to the Grand Banks, salt forming part of the ballast. Salt cod was then brought back as a valuable part of the winter diet

A coastal boat was built in 1948 and named the SS. Springdale, after the Community. For many people the S.S. Springdale was considered a lifeline for the remote communities it served and was a welcome sight following the harsh winter and ice conditions experienced each year. During its years in service, the SS Springdale was described as a workhorse that performed honorable service to coastal residents.

S.S. Springdale

The Compiler of these pages did not expect, when he commenced tracing the line of his ancestry, running back in some respects beyond the first immigrant of the name, to bring anything before the public.

After years of research, so much interesting material was discovered that I decided to continue. There turned out to be many more Family Members than originally though.

We should honor the memory of those who, gave us our being and their order of birth in the family is denoted by Roman numerals. Each person is numbered in common Figures, and the order of birth in the family by Roman Letters. This arrangement will enable one to know the branch which he or she is connected, and by referring back to the preceding generation in his or her branch, they will follow back the line to their ancestry.

Surnames from then Huxter Clan:

A --Abbott, Adams, Anderson, Andrews, Anstey, B - Bargery, Barnes, Beaton, Bellows,

Benko, Bennett, Bishop, Brenton, Brown, Budgell, Burr, Bursey, Burton, Butler,

C --Clarke, Cokes, Colbourne, Cook, Cooper, Corbett, Correll, Crosson, Croucher, Curtis,

D --Dalton, Davis, Dicks, Doody, Drake, Dunphy, Dwyer, E --Edgecombe, Ellsworth

England, F -- Ferguson, Fisher, Fitzpatrick, Fortin, Foss, Foster, Fowlow, Freake, Fudge,

G --Galardi, George, Gesner, Gilbert, Gillard, Gosse, Godard, Grant, Graves, Gregory, Gushue,

H --Hancock, Hann, Hayden, Heath, Hefferan, Henniffent, Henstridge, Hill, Hiscock,

Hobbs, Hopkins, House, Howell, Hussey, Hutcheson, Hutt, Huxter, I --Ireland

J --James, Jones, Joy, K --Kantor, Keefe, Kelly, Kiss, Knee, Knight, Knottnerus, Knowles,

L --Leagan, Lamphear, Lethbridge, Lloyd, Locke, Lodge, Lundrigan, Lush,

M --Macdonald, MacDougall, MacEwen, Macvicar, Manuel, Martin, Matthews, McKay,

McKenzie, McLean, Meilleur, Mercer, Metcalfe, Miller, Mombourquette, Moore, Morris,


Mouland, Moyle, Myer,

N --Noftle, Norman, Normore, Noseworthy, Nouasri, Oliver, Osmond, Oxford,

P --Pardy, Penny, Pheifer, Piercey, Pittman, Porter, Powell, Power, Prall, Prine,

Q –Quest, R—Rasenberger, Reid, Reynolds, Rice, Richards, Ricketts, Rideout,

Roberts, Rodgers, Rosser, Rowe, Rowsell, Ryall, Ryan,

S—Saunders, Schultz, Sears, Sevard, Sharron, Sheppard, Shiner, Short, Skinner, Slaney,

Smith, Snook, Spencer, Spice, Stacey, Stead, Stone, Stuckless, Sweeney

T –Tavares, Taylor, Tippett, Tizzard, Tucker, Tulk, V—Vincent,

W -- Walsh, Walters, Warren, Wells, Whalen, White, Wiesenberg, Wight, Williams, Wilson, Wilshire, Wiseman, Y –Yates, Young.

Nathaniel Huxter Samuel Huxter

Samuel was born in a fishing community on the southern shore of Fogo Island, Nfld in a place called Seldom-Come-By in 1872. Sarah was born at Grand Bank in Fortune Bay, on the Southern Coast of Nfld in 1886. Samuel (1) married Irene Penny from Springdale In 1899 and they had one son, Stephen born in 1900. Stephen lived at Badger, Nfld. Irene died in 1901. Samuel (2) married Sarah Hickman Hiscock.

Sarah received her teaching Certificate when she was only 15 years old in 1901.. Samuel and Sarah were married in George Brown’s house in Springdale in 1903, Sarah was only 17 years old. Samuel and Sarah had 11 children. All but one child was born at Springdale. In 1904 there was a Forest fire that burned Springdale to the ground. Sarah was pregnant in the summer of 1904 and she went back to Grand Bank to have her first child. Marion was born on Dec 3, 1904 at Grand Bank

In 1908 Sarah helped to organize the ladies aid and became secretary-treasurer. She was also very active within the United Church. Samuel and his brother Nathaniel worked in Sawmills in Hall’s Bay in 1898

In the mid 1920’s Samuel hurt his back and could not work in the woods or any heavy lifting, Nathaniel was the Warden at Indian Falls and he gave up his job so his brother could have it.

In 1928 grandfather became the warden and for the next 22 years he was regarded as one of Nfld’s most efficient wardens. He was an authority on wildlife and he took his job with great seriousness, impressing at all times upon sportsmen, the necessity of not only preserving game, but the forest as well. He was particularly careful about fire in the woods, and had a special fire place and galley for the use of all who came to the Falls. In his long period of service as game warden he won many friends. Fishman who went to the Falls not only brought away salmon with them, but many happy memories of the pleasant hours spent in his cheerful company. And should a fishman fail to get a salmon, as is all too often the case, he received a measure of philosophic comfort that helped make up for his disappointment.

The population of Springdale in 1908 was 373 made up of 24 Church of England, 341 Methodists, 3 Salvation Army and 1 Presbyterian, 1 pauper, 1 deaf mute, 1 clergyman and one teacher. The town had 63 dwelling for 65 families, 129 barns and outhouse, 130, acres of improved land on which was produced 68 tons of hay, 604 barrels of spuds and 120 barrels of turnips. The town also had 76 dogs employed in hauling wood, 17 horses, 35 cows, 10 bulls, 164 sheep, 36 pigs, 45 goats and 600 poultry. There were 3 sawmills employing 47 persons and the average income was $50.00 per head or $275.00 per family per year. $1.00 paid the light bill for the year (4 gal. of kerosene ) and the only thing the people wore on their feet was skin that came off the cow they killed the year before—and the wool that came off the sheep turned into knitted socks and stockings Life was Hard

Samuel And Sarah had a big house with a large kitchen and a big living room, one bed room down stairs and five bedrooms upstairs, three on the bay side and two on the other side. My grandmother kept goats, sheep, hens and ducks. In the twenties and thirties the land was used for vegetable and hay.
